content.assessment: This incremental approach has ambiguous fiscal-incidence implications: removing only the youngest entry point may not change the aggregate per-pupil funding disparity between tracks, since the two remaining entry points (age 12 and 14) still coexist with the SES-linked access mechanisms documented across the system (F-live-education_finance-03, F-live-education_finance-04). The fiscal magnitude of a partial reform is likely smaller than full restructuring, but the evidence base offers no proposal-specific cost data, and the risk of demand displacement toward the age-12 entry point could simply relocate—rather than reduce—the incidence problem, limiting value for money.
opportunities: Could serve as a lower-cost pilot to generate fiscal and equity data before committing to fuller restructuring
strengths: Lower fiscal magnitude than full structural reform, since only one of three entry points is affected
May reduce implementation costs relative to comprehensive restructuring given narrower scope
threats: Value for money is undermined if segregation dynamics persist essentially unchanged at the remaining age-12 and age-14 entry points (per the proposal's own risk framing), rendering the fiscal cost of reform poorly justified
weaknesses: Displaced demand to the age-12 track risks reproducing the same SES-linked access and funding-incidence problems documented for the full three-track system (F-live-education_finance-03)
No cost-effectiveness evidence exists distinguishing this partial approach from full delay-to-14 reform in fiscal terms
